Title: Changes in the allocation of endogenous strigolactone improve plant biomass production on phosphate-poor soils

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CaMV 35S promoter (constitutive) driving GFP-PDR1 overexpression; endogenous pPDR1 promoter used in reporter analyses in Arabidopsis for spatial expression
Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: W115 (Mitchell); pdr1 ko background W115 × W138
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: PDR1 is a strong engineering target to improve phosphate acquisition and biomass on phosphate-poor soils by enhancing endogenous strigolactone export, faster AMF colonization, and root architectural adaptation. Best deployment is in low-P soils, potentially combined with traits such as citrate overproduction or ABCG37/PDR9-mediated coumarin exudation. Use constitutive or root-active overexpression/editing strategies with caution in regions infested by parasitic weeds, because elevated SL exudation may stimulate their germination. Monitor possible drought/salinity tradeoffs due to larger leaf area and altered SL allocation.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes improved biomass production and phosphate uptake under phosphate-poor conditions by enhancing strigolactone transport/exudation, accelerating mycorrhization, increasing lateral root formation and root hair elongation, altering shoot morphology, and delaying leaf senescence.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Constitutive overexpression and knockout comparison; mycorrhization quantification (grid method and qPCR for fungal GiTUB markers); phosphate uptake measurement; biomass phenotyping; parasitic weed germination bioassay for SL activity; 3H-GR24 transport assays plus LC-MS/MS confirmation; qPCR of SL biosynthetic genes; X-ray computed tomography of roots; confocal microscopy with DR5:VENUS; root hair and lateral root phenotyping.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Low-phosphate versus higher-phosphate soils; clay, clay+, Claymin, Claymin+, natural soil mix, full soil, mineral soil; presence or absence of arbuscular mycorrhizal fungus Rhizophagus irregularis; sterile agar growth; high- versus low-Pi media; exogenous GR24 used in transport/bioassay experiments.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Previously reported and further supported here as active in root epidermal/export interfaces and shoot tissues; functional assays indicate strong roles in roots, leaves, stems, and leaf export routes. Time-lapse reporter analyses showed promoter activity in epidermal cells during root hair elongation.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: In Petunia, expressed in roots and shoots, including leaves/stems; promoter activity observed during root hair elongation and previously in nodes/axils.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PDR1 encodes an ABCG-class strigolactone transporter rather than a biosynthetic enzyme or TF, so it is classified here as a Common_Gene. Overexpression lowers detectable SL activity in roots and leaves while increasing DAD1/MAX1 expression, consistent with enhanced export relieving SL-dependent negative feedback on biosynthesis. The study proposes a new leaf-to-stem SL export route mediated by PDR1. PDR1 also alters auxin distribution in root tips indirectly, likely through SL-auxin crosstalk. Source species and applied species are the same in the main study; Arabidopsis reporters were used only for imaging support, not as the primary metabolic test species.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible increased stimulation of parasitic weed germination because of higher SL exudation; potential higher sensitivity to drought and salinity due to expanded leaf blade/higher transpiration; carbon cost of stronger mycorrhization may outweigh benefit under extremely poor soils with unfavorable AMF cost-benefit balance.
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: ABCG/PDR subfamily ATP-binding cassette transporter; pleiotropic drug resistance transporter
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PDR1 overexpression increased shoot and root biomass specifically on phosphate-poor substrates, especially when AMF colonization was beneficial. 2. PDR1 OE accelerated mycorrhization and enhanced phosphate uptake, with strong increases in fungal marker expression and mycorrhiza-inducible phosphate transporter expression. 3. PDR1 OE increased lateral root formation and root hair elongation even outside symbiotic conditions, indicating direct effects on root architecture. 4. PDR1 OE reduced SL accumulation in roots and leaves but upregulated DAD1 and MAX1, supporting a model in which SL export relieves negative feedback on biosynthesis. 5. PDR1 mediates leaf SL export and delays senescence, revealing a new leaf-to-stem transport route and linking SL allocation to nutrient-adaptive growth.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots, leaves, stems, and rhizosphere exudate
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Regulates shoot branching, root architecture, root hair elongation, mycorrhizal symbiosis initiation, phosphate-starvation adaptation, and leaf senescence.
